title: Gold Mid-Year Outlook 2026: Point break
gist: Gold soared above US$5,500/oz in January 2026 before falling below US$4,000/oz by June (down ~7% YTD), and the World Gold Council sees it staying rangebound near US$4,100/oz (±5%) in H2 unless a catalyst breaks it toward US$4,500/oz on the upside or below ~US$3,860/oz on the downside. Central bank buying, Asian price discovery, and Indian import-duty hikes are the key wildcards.

insights.claim: Gold set 12 all-time highs and crossed above US$5,500/oz intraday in late January 2026 before briefly dipping below US$4,000/oz in late June, ending roughly 7% down year-to-date.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: The ~28% peak-to-trough swing shows gold's elevated tail risk both ways; size positions for volatility, not a smooth trend.
insights.quote: gold set 12 all-time highs, surpassing US$5,500/oz intraday in late January 2026 amid heightened geopolitical risks and elevated options activity, before falling towards – and briefly dipping below – US$4,000/oz in late June.

insights.claim: Gold's realised volatility spiked above 50% at the onset of the US-Iran conflict and has since fallen below 30%, though it remains above its 20-year average of 17%.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: WGC's mean-reversion analysis implies vol should keep normalising, favouring option sellers and reducing risk-manager pressure to cut gold exposure.
insights.quote: This sharp price swing pushed realised volatility to more than 50%

insights.claim: Despite the pullback, gold remains one of the best-performing assets of the last 12 months while other asset classes play catch-up.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Relative strength argues gold is consolidating gains rather than topping; use dips as entry rather than exit.

insights.claim: The World Gold Council's Gold Return Attribution Model attributes gold's H1 price variability 24% to momentum, 17% to risk & uncertainty, 14% to FX opportunity cost, 12% to economic expansion, and only 3% to rates, with 30% unexplained by the model.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Momentum and risk sentiment, not rates, drove H1 gold; watch positioning and geopolitical risk gauges more than the Fed for near-term direction.

insights.claim: Gold reacts to four main drivers: economic expansion, risk and uncertainty, opportunity cost (yields and the US dollar), and momentum from short-term flows.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A reusable lens for decomposing any gold move into fundamental versus flow components.
insights.locator: Risk and momentum have driven performance

insights.claim: Intraday tick analysis shows gold's pullbacks generally occurred during US trading hours while rebounds occurred during Asian hours, underscoring Asia's growing role in price discovery.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Asian consumer and investor demand is now a structural price-support engine; monitor Asian session flows and Chinese/Indian demand as leading signals.
insights.quote: Many of the pullbacks occurred during US hours and, conversely, gold's rebounds generally occurred during Asian hours.

themes.theme: H2 fair value and scenario ranges

insights.claim: The World Gold Council's Gold Valuation Framework judges gold fairly valued and projects it trades ±5% around US$4,100/oz in H2 2026 if macro conditions do not materially change.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Base case is rangebound ~US$3,900-4,300/oz; fade extremes toward this anchor absent a catalyst.
insights.quote: gold may trade ±5% around US$4,100/oz during the second half of the year.
insights.locator: The gold price as a barometer

insights.claim: A clear upside catalyst could lift gold back toward US$4,500/oz, and only a strong, sustained signal would push it toward US$5,000/oz.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Defines the bullish breakout ladder; US$4,500 is the first target on a risk or dovish shock, US$5,000 needs conviction.
insights.quote: gold could resume its upward trend around US$4,500/oz, but only a strong, clear signal may push it sustainably towards US$5,000/oz.
insights.locator: Can gold resume its upward trend?

insights.claim: Consensus expects the Fed, Bank of England, Bank of Japan and ECB to raise policy rates before year-end, with the Fed hiking by October, US 2026 growth of 2.1% and US inflation peaking near 3.9% in Q2.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: This tightening/moderate-growth backdrop is already priced into gold; surprises versus it, not the hikes themselves, move price.
insights.locator: The gold price as a barometer

insights.claim: A 100-point monthly increase in the Geopolitical Risk (GPR) index has historically pushed gold prices up by 2.5%.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A quantifiable geopolitical beta for gold; use GPR spikes to size tactical hedge adds.
insights.quote: a 100-point monthly increase in the GPR index has historically pushed gold prices up by 2.5%.
insights.locator: Private credit markets are also creating

insights.claim: Gold failed to rally on the US-Iran Middle East turmoil, which the World Gold Council frames as an idiosyncratic exception rather than a breakdown in gold's safe-haven behaviour.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Don't extrapolate gold's muted Iran response into a lost hedge; the safe-haven bid likely reasserts on the next shock.
insights.quote: We believe that a combination of idiosyncratic factors made this event's impact on gold the exception rather than the rule.
insights.locator: And while gold did not react positively

insights.claim: Lower oil prices may no longer mean lower inflation because a resurgence of demand could make inflation sticky, a scenario that benefits gold as a persistent-inflation hedge.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Watch for the falling-oil/rising-yields divergence as a signal of sticky inflation that supports gold once it outperforms commodities.
insights.quote: lower oil prices may no longer mean lower inflation, as a resurgence of demand could make inflation sticky
insights.locator: In some ways, lower oil prices

insights.claim: Questions about Fed independence shook markets in early January and were one of the factors behind gold's rise, and a shift back to dovish rate expectations under Chairman Warsh would likely benefit gold.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Fed-independence and dovish-repricing risk are live gold catalysts; watch Warsh's guidance and Trump rate-cut pressure.
insights.quote: questions about Fed independence shook markets in early January and were one of the factors behind gold's rise

insights.claim: US bar and coin demand tends to rise under Democratic control, and the closely contested November 2026 midterms, with Democrats positioned to retake the House and a possible split Congress, could raise policy uncertainty supportive of gold.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A split-Congress outcome is a modest gold tailwind via policy uncertainty and US retail coin demand.
insights.quote: US bar and coin demand tends to rise under Democratic control.

insights.claim: Sovereign wealth funds, pension funds and endowments are increasing gold participation, and a 2025 China pilot programme now lets top insurance companies invest in gold, adding sticky buy-and-hold demand.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Structural long-term buyer expansion (notably Chinese insurers) provides a durable demand floor; a bullish multi-year signal.

insights.claim: Gold trading below approximately US$3,860/oz could trigger an additional leg down, as psychological and technical levels act as trigger points, with gold currently well above its ~US$3,520/oz two-year average.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: US$3,860 is the key technical support to watch; a break opens downside toward the two-year average near US$3,520.
insights.quote: if gold were to trade below ~US$3,860/oz it could experience an additional leg down

insights.claim: Since 1971 there have been eight episodes where gold fell more than 20% from a record high, with an average drawdown of 36% and median of 29%, and gold currently sits about 25% below its record high.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: History suggests drawdowns typically stabilise near 30%, implying limited incremental downside from current levels for buy-the-dip investors.
insights.quote: there have been eight episodes in which gold has dropped by more than 20% after reaching a record high, with an average drawdown of 36% and a median of 29%

insights.claim: The World Gold Council's scenario analysis suggests that if gold declines 10-15% from current levels, further downside would likely be limited because lower prices historically trigger buying across sectors.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Bargain-hunting demand caps the bearish scenario; the practical floor sits roughly 10-15% below current price.

insights.claim: Resilient US and global growth combined with reduced geoeconomic risk could shift investors into risk assets and curtail gold's risk premia, since much of gold's gains since 2025 stem from geopolitical risk.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A 'Goldilocks' risk-on regime is the main bearish threat; monitor geopolitical de-escalation and USD strength as sell signals.

themes.theme: Central bank demand as structural support

insights.claim: Central banks have bought an average of 1,000t of gold per year since 2022 and are expected to remain net buyers in 2026 despite some tactical Q1 selling and swaps.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Persistent official-sector buying is the core structural bull thesis; a marked deceleration is the key risk to watch.
insights.quote: Central banks have been an important contributor to gold's performance, having bought an average of 1,000t per year since 2022.

insights.claim: An additional 20t-30t of central bank reserve buying above the ~600t/year long-term average translates into roughly a 1% increase in the gold price, amplified by the positive signal it sends investors.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A concrete elasticity to convert central bank demand data into price impact; track quarterly reserve reports.
insights.quote: an additional 20t–30t increase in reserves above the long-term average of around 600t per year should translate into approximately a 1% increase in the gold price.

insights.claim: Gold exhibits strong returns in periods when the combined contribution of central banks and investment demand exceeds 30% of total demand.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Use the 30% combined-demand-share threshold as a regime indicator for strong forward gold returns.

themes.theme: India: import duties and demand shock

insights.claim: India, gold's second-largest market at 800t of net demand per year, sharply raised its gold import duty from 6% to 15% since early April 2026 to conserve FX reserves amid INR pressure from the US-Iran conflict's oil impact.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: The duty hike is a demand headwind unique to India; monitor Indian policy and INR as a swing factor in physical demand.
insights.quote: including a sharp duty increase – from 6% to 15% – and consumer-directed messaging aimed at curtailing gold purchases.

insights.claim: The World Gold Council's econometric analysis estimates India's import duty increase alone will cut jewellery, bar and coin demand by 50t-60t (about 10% year-over-year), an impact it believes is already reflected in the gold price.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: The known duty shock is priced in; the residual risk is further Indian economic deceleration hitting demand via the income effect.
insights.quote: the country's import duty increases alone will reduce jewellery, bar and coin demand by 50t–60t (or about 10% y/y).

insights.claim: Rising defaults on India's increasingly popular collateralised gold loans could boost gold supply and add downside pressure if the economy decelerates.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A second-order Indian supply risk to watch alongside demand; gold-loan default data could presage recycling supply.

insights.claim: The World Gold Council quantifies key gold sensitivities: a 25bp fall in the US 10-year yield equals a 1.75% price rise, a 1% CPI increase a 0.5% rise, a 100pt monthly GPR increase a 2.5% rise, a 10% Indian import-duty hike a 2% fall, and 20t-30t of central bank buying a 1% move.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A ready-made sensitivity table to translate any macro surprise into an approximate gold price move.
